perturbation.original_text: 1.5 Notwithstanding the aforementioned Confidential I n f o r m a t i o n s h a l l e x c l u d e information that: (i) is already in the public domain at the time of disclosure by the Discloser to the Recipient or thereafter enters the public domain without any breach of the terms of this Agreement; (ii) was already known by the Recipient before the m o m e n t o f d i s c l o s u r e (under evidence of reasonable proof or written record of such disclosure); (iii) is subsequently communicated to the Recipient without any obligation of confidence from a third party who is in lawful possession thereof and under no obligation of confidence to the Discloser; (iv) becomes publicly available by other means than a breach of the confidentiality obligations by the Recipient (not through fault or failure to act by the Recipient); (iv) is or has been developed independently by employees, consultants or agents of the Recipient (proved by reasonable means) without violation of the European IPR Helpdesk | Page 5 of 8 terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Confidential Information pertaining to the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.5 Notwithstanding the aforementioned Confidential Information shall exclude information that: (i) is already in the public domain at the time of disclosure by the Discloser to the Recipient or thereafter enters the public domain without any action by the Recipient; (ii) was already known by the Recipient before the moment of disclosure (without any requirement of proof or written record of such disclosure); (iii) is subsequently communicated to the Recipient without any obligation of confidence from a third party who is in lawful possession thereof and under no obligation of confidence to the Discloser; (iv) becomes publicly available by other means than a breach of the confidentiality obligations by the Recipient (not through fault or failure to act by the Recipient); (iv) is or has been developed independently by employees, consultants or agents of the Recipient (proved by reasonable means) without violation of the European IPR Helpdesk | Page 5 of 8 terms of this Agreement or reference or access to any Confidential Information pertaining to the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: By changing '(i) is already in the public domain at the time of disclosure by the Discloser to the Recipient or thereafter enters the public domain without any breach of the terms of this Agreement;' to '(i) is already in the public domain at the time of disclosure by the Discloser to the Recipient or thereafter enters the public domain without any action by the Recipient;' and '(ii) was already known by the Recipient before the m o m e n t o f d i s c l o s u r e (under evidence of reasonable proof or written record of such disclosure);' to '(ii) was already known by the Recipient before the moment of disclosure (without any requirement of proof or written record of such disclosure);' introduces conflicting definition with section 1.4. Because, the recipient now can claim that he already knows the Confidential Information, even without proof. Now, that the Recipient doesn't need to give any proof about knowing the confidential information before, affects his/her liability in the contract. This affect the Discloser, now need to provide evidence of the breachment, because, the other party can say, there is no liability on its end.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.4 The Recipient hereby acknowledge that the Confidential Information proprietary of the Discloser has been developed and obtained through great efforts and shall be regarded and kept as Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the conditions under which information is excluded from being considered confidential. Specifically, it removes the requirement for 'evidence of reasonable proof or written record' when claiming prior knowledge of the information. This directly contradicts section 1.4, which emphasizes that the Discloser's Confidential Information has been developed through great effort and must be kept confidential. By removing the burden of proof from the Recipient regarding prior knowledge, it weakens the protection afforded to the Discloser's Confidential Information and introduces a conflicting definition. The original text ensures the confidential information developed by the Discloser is protected. The changed text introduces a loophole that impacts the Recipient's liability and the Discloser's ability to enforce confidentiality, thus creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.2 The Recipient will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Recipient will inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and will ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will be responsible for ensuring that the obligations of confidentiality and non-use contained herein will be strictly observed and will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.2 The Recipient will not disclose and will keep confidential the information received, except to its employees, representatives or agents who need to have access to the Confidential Information for the purpose of carrying out their duties in connection with the permitted purposes specified in clause 2. The Recipient will inform them about the confidential quality of the information provided and will make a reasonable effort to ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential on the same terms as set forth in this Agreement. Hence the Recipient will only be responsible for the actions made in bad faith by its personnel representatives or agents.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'will ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential' to 'will make a reasonable effort to ensure that their agreement is obtained to keep it confidential' and 'will assume full liability for the acts or omissions made for its personnel representatives or agents' to 'will only be responsible for the actions made in bad faith by its personnel representatives or agents' introduces a contradiction with clause 3.10, where the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public. This weakens the protection for the Discloser, as it's now harder to hold the Recipient liable for breaches of confidentiality by their personnel.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the Recipient's obligation to ensure confidentiality by changing 'will ensure' to 'will make a reasonable effort' and limiting liability to 'actions made in bad faith'. This directly contradicts clause 3.10, which states that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if Confidential Information is disclosed, regardless of the Recipient's effort or intent, and is entitled to damages. The original text made the recipient fully liable and ensured agreement to confidentiality. The change undermines the 'irreparable damage' clause by making it harder to hold the Recipient liable for breaches, creating a strong in-text contradiction based on the Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored), including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may request it to the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.7 The Recipient shall make reasonable efforts to return or destroy all copies (in whatever form reproduced or stored), including all notes and derivatives of the Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement, upon the earlier of (i) the completion or termination of the dealings contemplated in this Agreement; (ii) or the termination of this Agreement; (iii) or at the time the Discloser may request it to the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'The Recipient shall promptly return or destroy' to 'The Recipient shall make reasonable efforts to return or destroy' introduces a contradiction with clause 3.6, where all Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain under the property of the Discloser. The Recipient is not prompt to deliver back the information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.6 All Confidential Information disclosed under this Agreement shall be and remain under the property of the Discloser and nothing contained in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring any rights to such Confidential Information on the Recipient. Principally, nothing in this Agreement shall be deemed to grant to the Recipient a licence expressly or by implication under any patent, copyright or other intellectual property right. The Recipient hereby acknowledges and confirms that all European IPR Helpdesk | Page 6 of 8 the existing and future intellectual property rights related to the Confidential Information are exclusive titles of the Discloser. For the sake of clarity based in good faith, the Recipient will not apply for or obtain any intellectual property protection in respect of the Confidential Information received. Likewise any modifications and improvements thereof by the Recipient shall be the sole property of the Discloser.
perturbation.justification: The original text states the Recipient 'shall promptly return or destroy' the Confidential Information, creating a clear obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'shall make reasonable efforts to return or destroy'. This introduces ambiguity and directly contradicts section 3.6, which states that all Confidential Information 'shall be and remain under the property of the Discloser'. The 'reasonable efforts' language in the changed text is incompatible with the Discloser's right to absolute ownership and control of their Confidential Information as stated in 3.6. The change impacts the Discloser's property rights, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years] term. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during the term shall remain in effect indefinitely, save otherwise agreed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.1 This Agreement shall remain in effect for a term of [number of months or years] term. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Recipient’s duty to hold in confidence Confidential Information that was disclosed during the term shall remain in effect for the same duration of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall remain in effect indefinitely' to 'shall remain in effect for the same duration of this agreement', this contradicts the intention of protecting the information 'indefinitely'. This weakens the protection and introduces ambiguity on how long information is protected.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated that the duty to hold confidential information remains in effect 'indefinitely'. The changed text modifies this to 'for the same duration of this agreement'. This creates a strong contradiction with paragraph 3.10, which anticipates 'irreparable damage' if confidential information is disclosed and grants the Discloser the right to seek injunctive relief and damages. If the confidentiality obligation expires after the agreement term, as suggested by the changed text, the Discloser's ability to seek remedies for breaches occurring after that term is undermined, directly contradicting the intention and enforceability of section 3.10. The explanation provided also states that this introduces ambiguity on how long the information is protected.

perturbation.original_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes] shall have jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.2 Applicable Law and Jurisdiction This Agreement shall be construed and interpreted by the laws of [choose the applicable law]. The parties may choose the court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes] which shall have jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'The court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes] shall have jurisdiction' to 'The parties may choose the court of [choose the jurisdiction to settle disputes] which shall have jurisdiction.' introduces contradiction if one part doesn't agree to pick the the choosen jurisdiction, because now is something negotiable. This creates an in-text contradiction with clause 3.10, where the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, because there is an inestability of where the complain will be sent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.10 The Recipient agrees that the Discloser will suffer irreparable damage if its Confidential Information is made public, released to a third party, or otherwise disclosed in breach of this Agreement and that the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against a threatened breach or continuation of any such a breach and, in the event of such breach, an award of actual and exemplary damages from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an element of negotiation regarding jurisdiction, whereas the original text prescribed a specific jurisdiction. The contradicted text (3.10) states that the Discloser is entitled to obtain injunctive relief from 'any court of competent jurisdiction'. By making the jurisdictional clause negotiable, the changed text creates an ambiguity and potential instability in where the Discloser can seek injunctive relief, directly contradicting the guarantee provided in 3.10. The original text provided a defined competent jurisdiction whereas the changed text allows for a choice which could lead to delays and disputes and thus contradicts the other text.
